title: Java Basics
description: Learn Java from scratch — variables, control flow, loops, and methods.

units.title: Java Foundations
units.description: Understand what Java is and write your very first programs.

lessons.title: What is Java?
lessons.description: Get to know Java — where it came from and how it runs.

exercises.title: How Java runs
exercises.description: Java compiles your source code into bytecode, then the JVM runs it on any platform.
exercises.question: What does JVM stand for?
exercises.explanation: The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) runs Java bytecode on any platform — this is why Java is 'write once, run anywhere'.

exercises.title: Java portability
exercises.description: Think about Java's 'write once, run anywhere' philosophy.
exercises.question: A Java program compiled on Windows can run on Mac or Linux without recompiling.
exercises.explanation: The JVM handles platform differences. Your bytecode runs on any machine that has a JVM installed.

exercises.title: Java file extension
exercises.description: Java source files and compiled files use different extensions.
exercises.question: What file extension do Java source code files use?
exercises.explanation: .java is for source code. When compiled, it produces a .class (bytecode) file.

exercises.title: Static typing
exercises.description: Java enforces types at compile time — this is called static typing.
exercises.question: In Java, you must declare the type of every variable (e.g. int, String).
exercises.explanation: Java is statically typed. Unlike Python or JS, types are checked at compile time.

exercises.title: Java compilation
exercises.description: Fill in the file extension that Java source code compiles into.
hints: Java compiles to bytecode stored in a file — the same extension as the compiled output.

lessons.title: Your First Program
lessons.description: Structure of a Java program and the main method.

exercises.title: The main method
exercises.description: Java always looks for main() to start your program.
exercises.question: Which line is the correct declaration of Java's main method?
exercises.explanation: The exact signature `public static void main(String[] args)` is required — Java looks for this to start execution.

exercises.title: Class and filename
exercises.description: Java has a strict rule about how class names relate to file names.
exercises.question: The public class name must match the filename (e.g., HelloWorld.java must contain class HelloWorld).
exercises.explanation: Java requires this. If they don't match, you'll get a compile error.

exercises.title: Class declaration
exercises.description: Fill in the access modifier that makes the class visible from anywhere.
hints: This access modifier is the most permissive — it allows access from any other class.

exercises.title: main method name
exercises.description: Fill in the name of the method Java calls to start your program.
hints: This four-letter method name is Java's entry point.

exercises.title: Hello, World!
exercises.description: Write the classic first program. Print `Hello, World!` exactly.
hints: Use System.out.println() to print a line.
The text goes inside double quotes: System.out.println("Hello, World!");

lessons.title: Printing Text
lessons.description: Learn System.out.println and System.out.print.

exercises.title: System.out.println
exercises.description: Fill in the missing part. The full path is: System -> out -> println.
hints: It's the standard output stream — the 'output' object on System.

exercises.title: Multiple println calls
exercises.description: Each call to println() prints on its own line. What will this program print?
hints: Each println() moves to a new line after printing.

exercises.title: println method
exercises.description: Fill in the method name that prints text AND moves to a new line.
hints: 'println' = print + line. It prints and moves to the next line.

exercises.title: print vs println
exercises.description: `print()` does NOT add a newline. What happens when two `print()` calls run back to back?
hints: print() stays on the same line — the two outputs join together.

exercises.title: Print three lines
exercises.description:
Print these three words, each on its own line:
`Java`
`is`
`fun`
hints: Call System.out.println() three times.
Each call should have a different string: "Java", "is", "fun".

lessons.title: Chapter 1 Review
lessons.description: Review everything from Chapter 1.

exercises.title: Correct print syntax
exercises.description: Review the correct way to print in Java.
exercises.question: Which of these correctly prints 'Hi' in Java?
exercises.explanation: Java uses System.out.println(). console.log is JavaScript, echo is PHP/Bash.

exercises.title: print vs println
exercises.description: There's an important difference between print() and println().
exercises.question: System.out.print() automatically moves to a new line after printing.
exercises.explanation: Only println() moves to a new line. print() stays on the same line.

exercises.title: Completing main
exercises.description: Fill in the missing method name to complete the entry point.
hints: This is the method name Java uses as the program entry point.

exercises.title: Counting output
exercises.description: Trace through this program and predict what it prints.

exercises.title: I love Java
exercises.description: Print exactly: `I love Java!`
hints: System.out.println("I love Java!");

units.title: Data and Variables
units.description: Store, retrieve, and compute with different types of data.

lessons.title: Primitive Types
lessons.description: Java has built-in types for numbers, decimals, characters, and booleans.

exercises.title: Whole numbers
exercises.description: Java has different types for different kinds of numbers.
exercises.question: Which type stores whole numbers (integers) in Java?
exercises.explanation: int stores whole numbers like 1, -5, 100. double stores decimals like 3.14.

exercises.title: double and decimals
exercises.description: Know which type handles decimal (floating-point) numbers.
exercises.question: A double can store decimal numbers like 3.14 or 2.5.
exercises.explanation: double is Java's default type for decimal numbers. float also works but is less precise.

exercises.title: Single character
exercises.description: One of Java's types stores exactly one character.
exercises.question: Which Java type stores a single character like 'A' or '?'?
exercises.explanation: char stores one character, written in single quotes: char grade = 'A';

exercises.title: Declare an int
exercises.description: Fill in the type name for a whole-number variable.
hints: Use the 3-letter type for whole numbers.

exercises.title: Declare a double
exercises.description: Fill in the type name for a decimal-number variable.
hints: The type for decimal numbers is 6 letters long.

lessons.title: Declaring Variables
lessons.description: How to create, name, and assign variables in Java.

exercises.title: Variable name
exercises.description: Fill in the variable name that matches what is used in println.
hints: The variable name must match exactly what appears in println.

exercises.title: Variable output
exercises.description: When you print a variable, Java prints its value — not its name.

exercises.title: boolean declaration
exercises.description: Fill in the type for a true/false variable.
hints: The type that holds true or false is 7 letters.

exercises.title: Reassigning variables
exercises.description: Once declared, a variable's value can be changed.
exercises.question: You can change the value of a variable after it has been declared.
exercises.explanation: Variables can be reassigned. Use `final` if you want a constant that cannot change.

exercises.title: Declare and print
exercises.description: Declare an `int` variable called `age` with value `20`, then print it.
hints: int age = 20; then System.out.println(age);

lessons.title: Arithmetic
lessons.description: Perform math operations with Java's arithmetic operators.

exercises.title: Addition
exercises.description: Predict the result of adding two int variables.

exercises.title: Modulus operator
exercises.description: The modulus operator gives the remainder after division.
hints: The operator that gives the remainder is %.

exercises.title: Integer division
exercises.description: In Java, dividing two ints truncates the decimal.
exercises.question: What is the result of `10 / 3` in Java when both are ints?
exercises.explanation: Integer division drops the decimal. 10 / 3 = 3 (not 3.33).

exercises.title: Modulus result
exercises.description: Predict the remainder from this division.
hints: 7 divided by 2 is 3 with remainder 1.

exercises.title: Sum and product
exercises.description: Given `a = 8` and `b = 5`, print their sum on one line, then their product on the next.
hints: Sum uses +, product uses *.

lessons.title: Chapter 2 Review
lessons.description: Review variables, types, and arithmetic.

exercises.title: Modulus result
exercises.description: Test your understanding of the % operator.
exercises.question: What is the result of `15 % 4`?

exercises.title: int vs double
exercises.description: Assigning the wrong type causes a compile error in Java.
exercises.question: `int x = 3.14;` is valid Java.
exercises.explanation: 3.14 is a double. You cannot assign a double to an int without casting.

exercises.title: Reassignment
exercises.description: Trace through this code.
hints: x starts at 10. Then x = 10 + 5 = 15.

exercises.title: Subtract and multiply
exercises.description: Declare `int x = 6` and `int y = 4`. Print `x - y`, then print `x * y`.
hints: Declare both variables, then use println twice.

units.title: Text and Strings
units.description: Work with text using Java's String type.

lessons.title: String Basics
lessons.description: How to declare and use String variables.

exercises.title: Declaring a String
exercises.description: String is a special type in Java — note the capital S.
exercises.question: How do you declare a String variable in Java?
exercises.explanation: String is capitalized in Java. It's a class, not a primitive type.

exercises.title: String type
exercises.description: Fill in the type name for a text variable.
hints: Remember: the S is capitalized.

exercises.title: String quotes
exercises.description: Java uses double quotes for String values.
exercises.question: String values in Java are surrounded by double quotes.
exercises.explanation: Double quotes delimit Strings. Single quotes are for char values.

exercises.title: Printing a String
exercises.description: When you print a String variable, Java prints its contents.

exercises.title: Declare and print a String
exercises.description: Declare a `String` variable called `city` with value `"Paris"` and print it.
hints: String city = "Paris"; then System.out.println(city);

lessons.title: String Concatenation
lessons.description: Join Strings together using the + operator.

exercises.title: Joining Strings
exercises.description: Java uses a specific operator to join Strings.
exercises.question: How do you join two Strings in Java?
exercises.explanation: The + operator concatenates Strings: "Hello" + " World" = "Hello World".

exercises.title: Concatenation result
exercises.description: Two Strings joined with + are merged into one.

exercises.title: String + number
exercises.description: When you concatenate a String with a number, Java converts the number to text.

exercises.title: Concatenate with variable
exercises.description: Fill in the operator used to join Strings.
hints: The same operator used for addition also joins Strings.

exercises.title: Full name
exercises.description: Print `firstName` and `lastName` joined with a space.
hints: Use + to join them: firstName + " " + lastName

lessons.title: String Methods
lessons.description: Built-in methods that let you inspect and transform Strings.

exercises.title: length() method
exercises.description: Strings have a built-in method to count their characters.
exercises.question: What does `name.length()` return for `name = "Alice"`?
exercises.explanation: "Alice" has 5 characters: A-l-i-c-e.

exercises.title: toUpperCase
exercises.description: toUpperCase() returns a new String with all letters capitalized.

exercises.title: Calling length()
exercises.description: Fill in the method that returns the number of characters in a String.
hints: This method returns the number of characters.

exercises.title: toLowerCase
exercises.description: toLowerCase() returns a new String with all letters lowercased.

exercises.title: Uppercase
exercises.description: Declare `String word = "hello"` and print it in uppercase.
hints: Call .toUpperCase() on the string variable.

lessons.title: Chapter 3 Review
lessons.description: Review Strings, concatenation, and String methods.

exercises.title: charAt
exercises.description: charAt() returns the character at a given index (starting from 0).
exercises.question: What does `"Hello".charAt(0)` return?
exercises.explanation: charAt returns a char. Index 0 is the first character — 'H'.

exercises.title: String equality
exercises.description: Comparing Strings in Java requires a special approach.
exercises.question: You should use `==` to compare the content of two String variables.
exercises.explanation: Use .equals() to compare String content. == checks object identity.

exercises.title: Concatenation review
exercises.description: Predict the output of this concatenation.

exercises.title: Greeting
exercises.description: Declare `String greeting = "Hello"` and `String target = "Java"`. Print them joined with a space and an exclamation mark.
hints: Concatenate: greeting + " " + target + "!"

units.title: Making Decisions
units.description: Control what your program does using booleans and if/else.

lessons.title: Booleans
lessons.description: The boolean type holds true or false — the foundation of all decisions.

exercises.title: Boolean values
exercises.description: Java's boolean type has exactly two valid values.
exercises.question: Which of these is a valid boolean literal in Java?
exercises.explanation: Java booleans are lowercase: `true` and `false`.

exercises.title: Boolean range
exercises.description: Unlike numbers, booleans are extremely limited in what they can hold.
exercises.question: A boolean variable can only hold two values: true or false.
exercises.explanation: boolean is the simplest type — just two states.

exercises.title: Declare a boolean
exercises.description: Fill in the type name for a true/false variable.
hints: The type for true/false values is 7 letters long.

exercises.title: Comparison result
exercises.description: Comparison operators like `>` produce a boolean result.
hints: 5 > 3 is true, so x is true.

exercises.title: Equality check
exercises.description: The `==` operator checks equality and returns a boolean.
hints: 10 is not equal to 20, so the result is false.

lessons.title: if / else
lessons.description: Run different code depending on a condition.

exercises.title: if keyword
exercises.description: Every conditional statement starts with a specific keyword.
exercises.question: What keyword starts a conditional statement in Java?
exercises.explanation: `if` is the keyword. The condition goes in parentheses: if (condition) { ... }

exercises.title: if keyword
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword that starts the conditional block.
hints: The 2-letter keyword that starts a condition check.

exercises.title: if/else branch
exercises.description: Only one branch executes — the one whose condition is true.
hints: 3 > 5 is false, so the else branch runs.

exercises.title: else if chain
exercises.description: else if lets you check multiple conditions in sequence.
hints: 75 is not >= 90, but it is >= 70, so 'B' is printed.

exercises.title: Positive or negative
exercises.description: Given `int n = -5`, print `"positive"` if n > 0, otherwise print `"negative"`.
hints: Use if (n > 0) { ... } else { ... }

lessons.title: Comparison Operators
lessons.description: Compare values using ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=.

exercises.title: Not equal
exercises.description: Java has a specific operator to check if two values are NOT equal.
exercises.question: Which operator checks if two values are not equal in Java?
exercises.explanation: != means 'not equal to'. For example, 5 != 3 is true.

exercises.title: Greater than or equal
exercises.description: The >= operator returns true if the left side is greater than or equal to the right.
hints: 5 >= 5 is true because they are equal.

exercises.title: Not equal operator
exercises.description: Fill in the operator that checks inequality.
hints: The not-equal operator is ! followed by =.

exercises.title: Chained comparisons
exercises.description: Each comparison returns a boolean. Trace through carefully.
hints: Evaluate each comparison separately.

exercises.title: Even or odd
exercises.description: Given `int n = 7`, print `"even"` if n % 2 == 0, otherwise print `"odd"`.
hints: Use the modulus operator: if (n % 2 == 0) it's even, otherwise odd.

lessons.title: Chapter 4 Review
lessons.description: Review booleans, if/else, and comparisons.

exercises.title: Boolean from comparison
exercises.description: Comparison operators always return a boolean.
exercises.question: The expression `10 > 3` evaluates to a boolean value.
exercises.explanation: All comparison operators (>, <, ==, !=, >=, <=) return true or false.

exercises.title: else if
exercises.description: Choose the correct syntax for chaining conditions.
exercises.question: Which is the correct syntax for an else-if in Java?
exercises.explanation: Java uses `else if` (two words) with parentheses around the condition.

exercises.title: Nested if
exercises.description: Trace through the nested conditions carefully.
hints: Both conditions are true: 8 > 5 and 3 < 5.

exercises.title: Grade checker
exercises.description: Given `int score = 85`, print `"pass"` if score >= 60, otherwise print `"fail"`.
hints: if (score >= 60) print pass, else print fail.

units.title: Loops
units.description: Repeat actions efficiently using for and while loops.

lessons.title: while Loop
lessons.description: Repeat a block of code as long as a condition is true.

exercises.title: while syntax
exercises.description: The while loop checks its condition before each iteration.
exercises.question: Which is the correct syntax for a while loop in Java?
exercises.explanation: while (condition) { } runs the body as long as condition is true.

exercises.title: while countdown
exercises.description: Trace through this while loop and predict what prints.
hints: i starts at 3, decrements each loop: 3, 2, 1. Stops when i reaches 0.

exercises.title: while keyword
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword that starts a while loop.
hints: The 5-letter keyword for this type of loop.

exercises.title: Infinite loop risk
exercises.description: If the condition never becomes false, the loop runs forever.
exercises.question: A while loop that never updates its condition variable will run forever.
exercises.explanation: Always make sure the loop variable changes so the condition eventually becomes false.

exercises.title: Print 1 to 5
exercises.description: Use a while loop to print the numbers 1 through 5, each on its own line.
hints: Start i at 1. Loop while i <= 5. Print i, then increment with i++.

lessons.title: for Loop
lessons.description: The for loop combines init, condition, and update in one line.

exercises.title: for loop parts
exercises.description: A for loop has three parts separated by semicolons.
exercises.question: In `for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)`, what does `i++` do?
options: Increments i by 1 after each iteration
exercises.explanation: i++ is the update step — it runs at the end of each iteration to move i forward.

exercises.title: for loop output
exercises.description: Trace through this for loop.
hints: i starts at 0, runs while i < 3: prints 0, 1, 2.

exercises.title: for loop condition
exercises.description: Fill in the condition to loop exactly 4 times (i = 0, 1, 2, 3).
hints: Use < to stop before reaching 4.

exercises.title: for loop sum
exercises.description: Trace through this accumulation loop.

exercises.title: Print even numbers
exercises.description: Use a for loop to print all even numbers from 2 to 10, each on its own line.
hints: Start at 2, go up by 2 each time: i += 2.

lessons.title: break and continue
lessons.description: Control loop flow with break and continue.

exercises.title: break keyword
exercises.description: break exits the loop immediately.
exercises.question: What does `break` do inside a loop?
exercises.explanation: break jumps out of the loop entirely. Execution continues after the closing brace.

exercises.title: break in action
exercises.description: Trace through and find where break stops the loop.
hints: The loop breaks when i equals 3, so only 0, 1, 2 are printed.

exercises.title: continue in action
exercises.description: continue skips the rest of the current iteration.
hints: When i == 2, continue skips that println. All other values print.

exercises.title: continue vs break
exercises.description: continue and break have different effects.
exercises.question: `continue` exits the loop entirely, while `break` skips just the current iteration.
exercises.explanation: It's the opposite: break exits the loop, continue skips the current iteration.

exercises.title: Skip multiples of 3
exercises.description: Print numbers 1 to 10, but skip any number divisible by 3.
hints: Use if (i % 3 == 0) continue; to skip multiples of 3.

lessons.title: Chapter 5 Review
lessons.description: Review while loops, for loops, break and continue.

exercises.title: for vs while
exercises.description: Both loops repeat code, but they suit different situations.
exercises.question: Which loop is best when you know exactly how many times to iterate?
exercises.explanation: for loops are ideal when the iteration count is known. while loops are better for unknown counts.

exercises.title: Loop review
exercises.description: Predict the output of this for loop.
hints: i starts at 10 and decrements. Loop runs while i >= 8.

exercises.title: Increment shorthand
exercises.description: Fill in the shorthand that increments i by 1.
hints: The post-increment operator adds 1 to i.

exercises.title: Multiplication table
exercises.description: Print the 3× table from 3×1 to 3×5. Each line: `3 x 1 = 3`, etc.
hints: System.out.println("3 x " + i + " = " + (3 * i));

units.title: Methods
units.description: Organise your code into reusable methods.

lessons.title: Defining Methods
lessons.description: How to write and call your own methods.

exercises.title: Method purpose
exercises.description: Methods let you group code into named, reusable blocks.
exercises.question: What is the main benefit of using methods?
exercises.explanation: Methods let you write logic once and call it from anywhere, avoiding duplication.

exercises.title: void method
exercises.description: A method that returns nothing uses the `void` return type.
hints: The return type for a method that returns nothing is 4 letters.

exercises.title: Calling a method
exercises.description: To run a method, call it by name with parentheses.
hints: sayHi() is called twice, so 'Hi!' prints twice.

exercises.title: Method reuse
exercises.description: A method can be called multiple times.
exercises.question: You can call the same method more than once in a program.
exercises.explanation: That's the whole point — write once, call many times.

exercises.title: Write a method
exercises.description: Define a static method `printLine()` that prints `"---"`, then call it 3 times from main.
hints: Define: static void printLine() { System.out.println("---"); }
Call it three times in main.

lessons.title: Parameters
lessons.description: Pass data into methods using parameters.

exercises.title: Parameters vs arguments
exercises.description: Parameters are defined in the method signature; arguments are passed when calling.
exercises.question: In `greet("Alice")`, what is `"Alice"`?
exercises.explanation: Arguments are the actual values you pass when calling. Parameters are the names in the definition.

exercises.title: Method with parameter
exercises.description: Trace through a method call with an argument.
hints: name takes the value of the argument each time greet() is called.

exercises.title: Parameter type
exercises.description: Fill in the parameter type for a whole number.
hints: The type for whole numbers is 3 letters.

exercises.title: Multiple parameters
exercises.description: Methods can take more than one parameter.

exercises.title: Square method
exercises.description: Define a static method `square(int n)` that prints `n * n`. Call it with 6.
hints: static void square(int n) { System.out.println(n * n); }

lessons.title: Return Values
lessons.description: Methods can compute and return a value to the caller.

exercises.title: return keyword
exercises.description: The return keyword sends a value back to the caller.
exercises.question: What keyword sends a value back from a method?
exercises.explanation: `return value;` ends the method and sends value back to whoever called it.

exercises.title: Return type
exercises.description: Fill in the return type for a method that gives back a whole number.
hints: The method returns a whole number — use the 3-letter type.

exercises.title: Using return value
exercises.description: The returned value can be stored or printed directly.
hints: multiply(4, 5) returns 4*5 = 20. result = 20, then printed.

exercises.title: void and return
exercises.description: void methods cannot return a value.
exercises.question: A method declared as `void` can use `return` to send back an int.
exercises.explanation: void means no return value. To return an int, the method must be declared as `int`.

exercises.title: Max of two numbers
exercises.description: Write a method `max(int a, int b)` that returns the larger of the two. Print the result of `max(7, 3)`.
hints: if (a > b) return a; else return b;

lessons.title: Chapter 6 Review
lessons.description: Review methods, parameters, and return values.

exercises.title: Method signature
exercises.description: The signature includes the name, parameter types, and return type.
exercises.question: Which part of a method definition specifies what type of value it returns?
options: The return type before the method name
exercises.explanation: The return type (e.g. int, void, String) appears before the method name.

exercises.title: Method chain
exercises.description: Trace through two method calls in sequence.
hints: square(4) returns 16. print(16) prints 16.

exercises.title: Complete the return
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword that sends a value back.
hints: The keyword that exits a method and sends a value back.

exercises.title: Celsius to Fahrenheit
exercises.description: Write a method `toFahrenheit(int c)` that returns `c * 9 / 5 + 32`. Print the result for `c = 100`.
hints: return c * 9 / 5 + 32;
